content.text:
The isomorphism theorems are the crown jewels of basic group theory. 
They reveal deep relationships between quotients, subgroups, and 
homomorphisms, providing powerful tools for analyzing group structure.

content.title: First Isomorphism Theorem
content.content:
Let $\phi: G \to H$ be a group homomorphism. Then:
\[
 G/\ker(\phi) \cong \text{Im}(\phi)
\]
More precisely, the map $\bar{\phi}: G/\ker(\phi) \to \text{Im}(\phi)$ 
defined by $\bar{\phi}(g\ker(\phi)) = \phi(g)$ is an isomorphism.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $K = \ker(\phi)$.

**Well-defined:** If $aK = bK$, then $b^{-1}a \in K$, so 
$\phi(b^{-1}a) = e_H$, hence $\phi(a) = \phi(b)$.

**Homomorphism:** $\bar{\phi}(aK \cdot bK) = \bar{\phi}((ab)K) = 
\phi(ab) = \phi(a)\phi(b) = \bar{\phi}(aK)\bar{\phi}(bK)$.

**Injective:** If $\bar{\phi}(aK) = e_H$, then $\phi(a) = e_H$, 
so $a \in K$, hence $aK = K$.

**Surjective:** For any $\phi(g) \in \text{Im}(\phi)$, we have 
$\bar{\phi}(gK) = \phi(g)$.

content.title: Applying the First Isomorphism Theorem
content.problem:
The determinant map $\det: GL_n(\R) \to \R^*$ has kernel $SL_n(\R)$. 
Since $\det$ is surjective onto $\R^*$:
\[
 GL_n(\R)/SL_n(\R) \cong \R^*
\]

content.title: Second Isomorphism Theorem
content.content:
Let $H \leq G$ and $N \trianglelefteq G$. Then:

1. $HN = \{hn : h \in H, n \in N\}$ is a subgroup of $G$

2. $H \cap N \trianglelefteq H$

3. $HN/N \cong H/(H \cap N)$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Define $\phi: H \to HN/N$ by $\phi(h) = hN$. This is a homomorphism. 
Its kernel is $\{h \in H : hN = N\} = H \cap N$. Since $\phi$ is 
surjective onto $HN/N$, the First Isomorphism Theorem gives the result.

content.title: Third Isomorphism Theorem
content.content:
Let $N \trianglelefteq G$ and $K \trianglelefteq G$ with $N \leq K$. Then:
\[
 (G/N)/(K/N) \cong G/K
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Define $\phi: G/N \to G/K$ by $\phi(gN) = gK$. This is a well-defined 
surjective homomorphism with kernel $K/N$.

content.title: Correspondence Theorem (Fourth Isomorphism Theorem)
content.content:
Let $N \trianglelefteq G$. There is a bijection between:

- Subgroups of $G$ containing $N$

- Subgroups of $G/N$
given by $H \mapsto H/N$ (where $N \leq H \leq G$). This bijection 
preserves normality, indices, and containment.
content.label: Theorem

items: Use the First Isomorphism Theorem to prove $\Z/n\Z \cong \Z_n$.

Apply the Second Isomorphism Theorem with $H = \langle (1\ 2) 
 \rangle$ and $N = A_3$ in $S_3$.

Use the Third Isomorphism Theorem to show 
 $(\Z/6\Z)/(\langle 2 \rangle/6\Z) \cong \Z/\langle 2 \rangle 
 \cong \Z_2$.

List all subgroups of $\Z_{12}/\langle 4 \rangle$ and identify 
 the corresponding subgroups of $\Z_{12}$.

marginNotes.text:
For just as the body is one and 
has many members, and all the members of the body, though many, are 
one body, so it is with Christ.
